metadata.career_relevance: Lambdas are the most important syntax change in Java 8 — and Java 8 is still the most widely deployed version in enterprise backends. Every modern Java codebase uses lambdas constantly: list.sort(comparator), list.forEach(action), stream.filter(predicate). You cannot read a modern Spring Boot codebase without understanding lambdas. Before lambdas, Comparator required 5 lines of anonymous class boilerplate. After lambdas: one line. After this topic, you read and write lambdas fluently — the foundation for streams in Topics 8.3-8.5.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll write lambdas in all their forms (single expression, block body, no parameters, multiple parameters), understand what a functional interface is, use lambdas with Comparator and forEach, understand variable capture (effectively final), and be ready for the Stream API.

concept_explanation.what_are_lambdas:
A **lambda expression** is an anonymous function — a function without a name that can be passed as a value.

```java
// Lambda: (parameters) -> body
(int x, int y) -> x + y          // takes two ints, returns sum
(String s)     -> s.toUpperCase() // takes String, returns uppercase
()             -> System.out.println("hello")  // no params, no return
(Product p)    -> p.getPrice() > 1000  // returns boolean
```

A lambda is NOT a method — it has no name, no access modifier, no return type declared. It's a value you can pass to a method or store in a variable.

**What can lambdas replace?** Any place Java expected an object whose class has exactly ONE abstract method — a **functional interface**. The lambda IS that one method's implementation.
concept_explanation.variable_capture:
**Variable capture** — lambdas can use variables from their enclosing scope:

```java
String prefix = "Hello";
List<String> names = Arrays.asList("Aisha", "Raj");

// Lambda captures 'prefix' from outer scope
names.forEach(name -> System.out.println(prefix + ", " + name));
```

**The rule**: captured variables must be **effectively final** — either declared `final` or never modified after initial assignment:

```java
String prefix = "Hello";    // effectively final (never reassigned)
names.forEach(name -> System.out.println(prefix + ", " + name));  // OK

prefix = "Hi";              // NOT effectively final (reassigned)
names.forEach(name -> System.out.println(prefix + ", " + name));  // COMPILE ERROR
```

**Why**: lambdas might execute later (in another thread). If the variable could change, the lambda would see an inconsistent value. Java enforces effectively-final to prevent this subtle bug.

visual_aid.description: Show three things: (1) Lambda syntax breakdown — annotated arrow diagram: (params) -> body. Parameters on left, arrow in middle, body on right. Three forms shown: no-params `() -> ...`, single-param `x -> ...`, block `(x,y) -> { ...; return ...; }`. (2) Functional interface + lambda connection — interface box with one abstract method, arrow labeled 'lambda IS this method'. (3) Before/after comparison: 5-line anonymous Comparator vs 1-line lambda. Side arrows pointing to the same output (they're equivalent). Bottom: variable capture — effectively final rule with visual of variable being 'locked' after first assignment.
visual_aid.alt_text: Lambda syntax forms, functional interface connection, and before/after anonymous class comparison

worked_example.scenario: Upgrade the Product catalog sort system from Topic 5.5 (which used anonymous class Comparators) to use lambdas. Show every anonymous class being collapsed to a lambda, and demonstrate the power of composing Comparators with lambda-based expressions.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Refactor `ProductSorter` from anonymous classes to lambdas. Show 5 sort variations:
1. By price ascending
2. By price descending
3. By name alphabetical
4. By category then price
5. By discounted price (computed field — shows lambda calling method)

step_by_step_solution.action: Using the lambdas with list.sort() and forEach

worked_example.expected_output:
=== By Price ===
USB Hub      ₹499
Java Book    ₹649
Mouse        ₹799
Keyboard     ₹1999

=== By Final Price ===
USB Hub      ₹374.25 (25% off)
Java Book    ₹551.65 (15% off)
Mouse        ₹719.10 (10% off)
Keyboard     ₹1599.20 (20% off)

=== Alphabetical ===
Java Book
Keyboard
Mouse
USB Hub
things_to_notice: Each Comparator went from 5 lines (anonymous class) to 1 line (lambda). The logic is IDENTICAL — the boilerplate simply vanished.
`byFinalPrice` calls `a.finalPrice()` — a method on the Product object. The lambda has full access to all Product methods via the parameter.
`catalog.forEach(p -> ...)` replaces `for (Product p : catalog)`. Cleaner for simple iteration. The `p ->` names the variable, just like a for-each loop variable.
`System.out.printf` inside forEach: the lambda body can be any valid Java expression or block.
All three sort criteria produce different orderings of the same 4 products — same objects, same list, different Comparators. This is the power of passing behavior as a value.
The sort results are cumulative — each `catalog.sort()` reorders the existing list.

industry_examples.use_case: Spring Boot uses lambdas everywhere: routing (`.route(GET('/users'), req -> ...)` in functional endpoints), security configuration (`.authorizeRequests(auth -> auth.antMatchers('/admin').hasRole('ADMIN'))`), reactive programming (`.flatMap(user -> fetchOrders(user))`). You cannot read a modern Spring Boot codebase without lambda fluency.

Android button click handlers went from 5-line anonymous classes to one-line lambdas: `button.setOnClickListener(view -> startActivity(intent))`. Adapter ViewHolder bind methods: `items.forEach(item -> holder.bind(item))`. Lambdas are the standard Android style since SDK 24+.

Search result sorting: `results.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(b.getRelevanceScore(), a.getRelevanceScore()))`. Filter pipelines: `products.removeIf(p -> !p.isInStock())`. These one-liners replace what used to be separate method implementations.

applications.interview_relevance:
Lambda questions at every Java developer level:

- **'What is a lambda expression?'** — Anonymous function; syntactic shorthand for implementing a functional interface.
- **'What is a functional interface?'** — Interface with exactly one abstract method. `@FunctionalInterface` annotation enforces it.
- **'What is effectively final?'** — Variable never reassigned after initial assignment. Required for lambda capture.
- **'How do you sort a List<Employee> by salary descending?'** → `list.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(b.getSalary(), a.getSalary()))`
- **'How is a lambda different from an anonymous class?'** → Lambda has no `this`, no new instance, concise syntax. Functionally equivalent for single-method interfaces.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily lambda usage:

- Every list sort: `list.sort((a, b) -> ...)`
- forEach for side effects: `list.forEach(item -> process(item))`
- removeIf: `list.removeIf(item -> condition)` (from Topic 6.1 — now you know WHY it takes a lambda)
- Map.forEach: `map.forEach((k, v) -> ...)`
- Event handlers, callbacks, Spring security config
- Reading code: every `->` in a codebase is a lambda — fluent reading is critical

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Trying to modify a captured variable inside a lambda — compile error.
common_gaps.remediation: Variables captured by lambdas must be effectively final (never reassigned). To accumulate values, use an array trick (`int[] count = {0}; list.forEach(x -> count[0]++)`) or use streams with reduction.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: 'Local variable X defined in an enclosing scope must be final or effectively final'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using block lambda `{...}` but forgetting the explicit `return` statement.
common_gaps.remediation: Expression lambda (`(a,b) -> expr`) — implicit return. Block lambda (`(a,b) -> { ...; }`) — must have explicit `return`. Compile error if missing.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Trying to assign a lambda to a non-functional interface type.
common_gaps.remediation: Lambdas only work with functional interfaces (exactly one abstract method). The type on the left side determines which functional interface the lambda implements.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: 'The target type of this expression must be a functional interface'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Not knowing when to include/omit parentheses around parameters.
common_gaps.remediation: Zero or two+ parameters → always parentheses: `() -> ...`, `(a, b) -> ...`. Single parameter → optional: `x -> ...` or `(x) -> ...`. Both forms work.

key_takeaways: Lambda: anonymous function passed as a value. Syntax: `(params) -> body`
Functional interface: exactly one abstract method. Lambda = implementation of that method.
Three forms: expression `(a,b) -> a+b`, block `(a,b) -> { ...; return ...; }`, no-params `() -> expr`
Type inferred from context — don't need to declare parameter types in most cases
Single parameter: parentheses optional (`x -> expr` or `(x) -> expr`)
Block body requires explicit `return`. Expression body: implicit return.
Lambda vs anonymous class: equivalent, lambda is cleaner, lambda's `this` = enclosing class
Variable capture: captured variables must be effectively final (never reassigned)
Method reference `::` is even shorter when lambda just calls one method (Topic 8.2)

self_check_questions.question:
What does this lambda do and what type is it?
`(String s) -> s.length() > 5`
self_check_questions.answer: It takes a String parameter and returns a boolean — whether the string length is greater than 5. The type is `Predicate<String>` (functional interface with `boolean test(T t)`). It could also be used anywhere a functional interface with `boolean f(String s)` signature is expected.

self_check_questions.question:
Why does this fail to compile?
```java
int count = 0;
list.forEach(item -> count++);  // compile error
```
self_check_questions.answer: The lambda tries to modify `count`, making it NOT effectively final. Lambdas can only capture variables that are never reassigned after initial assignment. Fix 1: use an array: `int[] count = {0}; list.forEach(item -> count[0]++)`. Fix 2: use Stream.count() or Collectors.counting() (covered in Topics 8.3-8.4) which don't require a mutable variable.

`() -> System.out.println('Hello')`

Runnable has one abstract method: `void run()`. No parameters, no return value. The lambda body is the print statement. No parentheses around the body since it's a single statement (though `() -> { System.out.println('Hello'); }` also works).

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Functional Interfaces** — the four standard functional interfaces Java provides: `Predicate<T>` (boolean test), `Function<T,R>` (transform), `Consumer<T>` (act, no return), `Supplier<T>` (produce, no input). Once you know these, you can use lambdas in ANY context, not just Comparator and forEach. Then Topic 8.3 opens the Stream API — where all of this comes together into a powerful data processing pipeline.
